## Climate Effects on Abundance and Distribution:

Fluctuations in Atlantic chub mackerel abundance have been reported in the Mid-Atlantic and New England, and may result from environmental influences such as temperature and upwelling. Oceanographic processes likely influence recruitment and distribution, as all life stages of chub mackerel are fully pelagic (Hernandez and Ortega 2000). In the eastern Atlantic, chub mackerel distributions have shifted northward, with a delayed and prolonged spawning season with increasing latitude (Domínguez-Petit et al. 2022). In the Alboran sea, chub mackerel abundance is vulnerable to variations in sea surface temperature, salinity, currents, and wind patterns (Ayman et al. 2024).

## Life History Synopsis:

Atlantic chub mackerel are a schooling pelagic species ranging from Nova Scotia to Argentina, including the Gulf of America, and are found on the continental shelf at depths of 250-300 m (MAFMC 2025). They are batch spawners, spawning January through April from North Carolina through the Gulf of America (Daley 2018). Spawning generally occurs in waters greater than 10°C and is possibly triggered by water temperatures between 15- 20°C (Hernandez and Ortega 2000). Chub mackerel eggs develop for approximately 53-56 hrs at 18-20°C. Larval stages last between 16-24 days, developing faster at higher temperatures (Hernandez and Ortega 2000). Atlantic chub mackerel grow rapidly during the first year of life, reaching maturity around 2 years of age (Daley 2018) and can live up to 13 years (Hernandez and Ortega 2000). Chub mackerel are generalist predators of zooplankton and other pelagic prey including mysids, euphausids, decapod larvae, copepods, amphipods, small fish, cephalopods. Cannibalism has been observed in this species (Hernandez and Ortega 2000). There is little data on chub mackerel predation in the Northwest Atlantic. The Mid-Atlantic Fishery Management Council funded a study on diet composition of marlins and tunas and found very few chub mackerel in the stomachs of yellowfin tuna and white marlin (MAFMC 2025). In 2019, the Mid-Atlantic Fishery Management Council approved Amendment 21 to the Mackerel, Squid, and Butterfish Fishery Management Plan. This Amendment established conservation and management measures for Atlantic chub mackerel in the Northwest Atlantic (MAFMC 2019). There is currently no stock assessment of chub mackerel in the Mid-Atlantic. Biomass is assumed to be at a sustainable level based on stock productivity and low fishery capacity (MAFMC 2025).
